flagged_claims.explanation: Source note mandates potassium recheck in 2 hours with urgent nephrology referral and possible dialysis; a 15-day follow-up directly contradicts the documented urgent monitoring plan and poses immediate patient safety risk.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Critical hyperkalemia: Potassium 6.8 mEq/L (critical threshold >6.0 mEq/L), explicitly marked as CRITICAL VALUE with physician call required
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Potassium: 6.8 mEq/L ⚠️ CRITICAL VALUE - CALL PHYSICIAN

flagged_claims.claim_text: Severe hyperkalemia diagnosis with identified etiology (spironolactone + ACEI + CKD interaction)
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Severe hyperkalemia (probably secondary to spironolactone + ACEI + CKD)

flagged_claims.claim_text: ECG changes consistent with hyperkalemia: peaked T waves in precordial leads indicating active cardiac risk
flagged_claims.source_evidence: ECG: Peaked T waves in precordial leads. No arrhythmias. Incipient electrocardiographic changes

flagged_claims.claim_text: Dangerous drug interaction: Spironolactone + Losartan (ACEI/ARB) + CKD contributing to life-threatening hyperkalemia; immediate discontinuation of spironolactone ordered
Immediate IV cardioprotective and potassium-lowering treatment initiated: Calcium gluconate IV + Insulin/Dextrose IV
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Calcium gluconate IV 1 ampule (cardioprotection). Regular insulin 10U + Dextrose 50% 50ml IV

flagged_claims.claim_text: Urgent nephrology referral required with consideration of dialysis if no treatment response
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Urgent nephrology referral. Consider dialysis if no response

flagged_claims.claim_text: Potassium recheck mandated in 2 hours — summary incorrectly implies routine 15-day follow-up, directly contradicting urgent monitoring plan

aegis_export.flawed_summary: Male patient, 72 years old, with chronic kidney disease, diabetes, and hypertension. Mild anemia (Hb 11.2). Compromised renal function (creatinine 2.1). Follow-up in 15 days.
aegis_export.corrected_summary: Male patient, 72 years old, with chronic kidney disease stage 3b, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension. Mild normocytic anemia (Hb 11.2 g/dL). Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (elevated, consistent with patient's baseline). CRITICAL VALUE: Potassium 6.8 mEq/L ⚠️ — physician call required. ECG shows peaked T waves in precordial leads (no arrhythmias), indicating incipient electrocardiographic changes consistent with hyperkalemia and active cardiac risk. Diagnosis: severe hyperkalemia, probably secondary to spironolactone + ARB (Losartan) + CKD interaction. URGENT actions taken/ordered: (1) Spironolactone discontinued immediately; (2) Calcium gluconate IV 1 ampule for cardioprotection; (3) Regular insulin 10U + Dextrose 50% 50ml IV for potassium shift; (4) Potassium recheck in 2 hours; (5) Urgent nephrology referral; (6) Dialysis to be considered if no treatment response.
